item.title: Continuous Sense-FET Current Measurement in a GaN-Based T-Type Bridge-Leg With Monolithic Bidirectional Switch
item.description: This letter introduces a GaN-based T-type bridge-leg using three GaN power ICs, including a monolithic bidirectional switch, with a novel sense-FET-based method for continuous phase current measurement. Experimental results at 200 V demonstrate the current sensing up to ±4 A across all four operating quadrants. The approach could enable high-bandwidth, nearly lossless current measurement, and is applicable to various converter topologies.

item.title: Multiphase LLC Resonant Converter With Interleaving, Natural Current-Sharing, and Phase-Shedding
item.description: A plethora of research has been conducted on ensuring reliable operation of multiphase LLC resonant converters, particularly addressing the uneven current distribution caused by mismatched circuit parameters. However, most existing solutions achieve current-sharing (CS) and phase-shedding (PS) at the cost of additional components and increased system complexity. This article proposes a novel interleaved multiphase LLC resonant converter that simultaneously achieves CS, PS, and interleaving without the need for extra components and added cost. This is made possible by a common voltage doubler rectifier (CVDR), which not only reduces the number of voltage-doubler capacitors but also acts as a variable voltage source to ensure excellent CS. In addition, the CVDR facilitates seamless PS and interleaving operations, leading to enhanced dynamic response, reduced input-output filter capacitances, and improved light-load efficiency. The operating principles of CS and PS are discussed, and a 750 W three-phase hardware prototype is developed to experimentally validate the performance of the proposed multiphase LLC converter.

item.title: N-Stage Phase-Interleaved Fourth-Order LCLC Resonant Converter for High-Voltage DC Applications
item.description: A single-stage LCLC resonant converter (LCLC-RC)-based high-voltage (HV) generator is limited in very high voltage (VHV) applications (hundreds of kilovolts) due to restricted component voltage ratings, impractical step-up ratios of the high-voltage high-frequency transformer (HVHFT), severe HVHFT parasitics, and increased AC insulation stress. Increasing the number of stages raises the output voltage, but also increases the output ripple amplitude, requiring larger filters with higher stored energy, which is critical for arc-sensitive loads such as klystron, electron gun, and other high-power RF amplifiers. To address these challenges, this letter proposed an N-stage phase-interleaved fourth-order LCLC-RC for VHV applications. The proposed converter delivers N times higher output voltage compared to a single-stage, while phase interleaving increases the ripple frequency by N, which reduces output ripple and stored energy. Furthermore, all parasitic elements of the HVHFT are absorbed into the resonant tank, enabling unity power factor (UPF) operation and reducing circulating current. A −50 kV, 800 W laboratory prototype validates the concept, achieving low ripple (≈0.036%), UPF operation, soft switching, and a peak efficiency of 95.26%, demonstrating its suitability for compact and efficient HV power supplies.

item.title: Multirung Ladder Wireless Power Transfer System for Stackable Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Swarm Charging Applications
item.description: This letter proposes a magnetic-circuit co-design for a wireless power transfer system to facilitate autonomous charging of vertically stacked unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) clusters. To address the challenges of position-dependent coupling and unstable power distribution in multiload scenarios, a multirung axial magnetic coupler is designed, ensuring stable coupling with lightweight receivers regardless of their vertical stacking position, thereby effectively mitigating issues related to position-dependent power transfer. By integrating this coupler with an LCL-S compensation network, the system achieves load-independent constant-current excitation. In this configuration, the total output power scales linearly with the number of active UAVs, while individual power delivery remains invariant to vertical positions. A 250-W experimental prototype validated the design, achieving stable concurrent charging for multiple units with a DC–DC efficiency of 86.3%, thus offering a robust and scalable solution for high-density UAV swarm operations.

item.title: Impedance-Aware Optimal Current Control for Grid-Forming Railway Traction Four-Quadrant Converters
item.description: This letter proposes an optimal current control scheme for grid-forming (GFM) four-quadrant converters (4QCs) designed to overcome power limitation and voltage drop issues in long-distance railway traction power supply systems. The proposal utilizes the reactive power generation capability of 4QCs and the voltage-source characteristic of GFM control to effectively support the line voltage. The analytical results reveal the relationship between equivalent impedance, grid voltage, and reactive power injection, which proves the optimal current and the impedance-awareness feature of the proposal. To make the proposal implementable in real-world 4QCs, a hill climbing search algorithm is introduced to track the optimal line current. The proposal is validated through experiments.

item.title: Voltage and Power Sharing in $LLC$-Based DC/DC Stages of Solid-State Transformer Architectures
item.description: This letter investigates the power sharing behavior of the DC–DC stage in two main modular solid-state transformer architectures: modular multilevel converter (MMC)-based and cascaded H-bridge (CHB)-based systems. The DC–DC power module is considered to be an $LLC$ resonant converter operating as a DC transformer. An analytical approach is developed to characterize the impact of component mismatch on module input voltages and power distribution. The analysis enables direct evaluation of the influence of DC–DC stage parameters and identifies the transformer turns ratio $n_{T}$ as the dominant factor governing imbalance. In MMC-based systems, all modules remain active under $n_{T}$ deviations, although voltage and power imbalances occur. In contrast, CHB-based systems are more sensitive to variations in $V_{i}$ and $n_{T}$, and adverse conditions may block power transfer. Experimental results validate the analysis and confirm the accuracy of the developed study.

item.title: An Above-Resonant-Frequency Phase-Shift Modulation for Wide Gain and Low Reactive Circulating Current Loss LLC Converters
item.description: This article proposes an above-resonant-frequency phase-shift modulation (ARFPSM) method suitable for LLC converters. Compared to pulse frequency modulation (PFM) methods, the proposed method can achieve the same gain with a narrower switching frequency range under high quality factor Q conditions. Furthermore, the proposed method achieves zero-voltage switching and zero-current switching across the entire gain range, while significantly reducing reactive circulating current and achieving higher overall efficiency. The precise relationship between soft-switching current, gain, frequency, and phase shift angle is established by the time-domain analysis method. Experimental results show that this method can achieve a wide input voltage range of 100–400 V, with a peak efficiency of up to 95.6% and an overall efficiency exceeding 93.7% at full load.

item.title: A Frequency-Selective Hybrid-Core Architecture for Third-Harmonic Loss Optimization in Wireless Power Transfer Systems
item.description: This letter proposes a frequency-selective hybrid-core architecture to improve the DC–DC efficiency of a 350-kHz wireless power transfer system. In this design, the power from the fundamental frequency is transmitted through a low-frequency core with high permeability magnetics (e.g., PC95), while the third-harmonic power is transmitted through a high-frequency magnetic material (e.g., PC200). By allocating spectral components to magnetic materials according to their frequency characteristics, the proposed hybrid-core configuration reduces third-harmonic-related loss in the main magnetic path and alleviates thermal stress in the magnetic components. A mathematical model has been established to verify the validity of the proposed method. A 10-kW prototype was built alongside a conventional system for comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves a peak efficiency of 98.22%, compared with 97.96% for the conventional system. These findings indicate a viable pathway to further efficiency enhancement even when conventional designs are widely regarded as highly optimized.

item.title: An Ultrafast Indirect Power Detection Based Short-Circuit Protection Method for GaN HEMTs
item.description: This letter proposes a novel indirect power detection based short-circuit protection (SCP) method for gallium nitride high-electron-mobility transistors (GaN HEMTs). The proposed method requires sensing only ${{{\bm{v}}}_{\mathbf{ds}}}$ of a GaN HEMT, similar to the widely used desaturation protection method. However, unlike desaturation protection, the proposed method directly monitors ${{{\bm{v}}}_{\mathbf{ds}}}$ after a predefined delay without a diode, enabling faster short-circuit detection and improved noise immunity. Experimental results show that the short-circuit detection time is significantly shorter than that of the desaturation protection. In addition, the proposed method is evaluated over a wide temperature range, from room temperature to cryogenic temperatures, verifying its effectiveness for GaN HEMTs in cryogenic applications.

item.title: Integrated Harmonic Mitigation and Fault Current Limiting for Grid-Forming Inverters
item.description: In microgrids with high penetration of power electronics-interfaced sources, harmonic distortion from nonlinear loads degrades the power quality at the terminals of virtual synchronous generators (VSGs). Conventional impedance-reshaping methods mitigate harmonics by reducing output impedance but often exacerbate fault currents during voltage sags. This letter proposes an integrated control strategy for harmonic mitigation and fault current limiting based on virtual forced excitation control (VFEC). The scheme extracts harmonic components from the point of common coupling (PCC) voltage and directly superimposes them onto the amplitude command of the internal electromotive force of the VSG. Under normal operation, this mechanism reduces the equivalent output impedance at harmonic frequencies, thereby attenuating nonlinear harmonic content and improving PCC voltage quality. During grid faults, the same architecture leverages inherent amplitude limiting to prevent overmodulation without control mode transitions or auxiliary circuits, effectively restricting fault currents to levels comparable to conventional VSGs and significantly below those of impedance-reshaping approaches. The proposed strategy ensures full compatibility with standard VSG control frameworks. Experimental results validate its effectiveness in both grid-connected and islanded microgrids.

item.title: Asymmetric Voltage Rating Pairing in IGCT-IGBT Hybrid Switch to Maximize Voltage Utilization for Cost-Effective MMC Applications
item.description: Modular multilevel converter (MMC) is cascaded by numerous submodules to support the DC bus voltage and generate the AC output voltage, resulting in its substantial manufacturing cost. In comparison to insulated-gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) and integrated gate-commutated thyristor (IGCT), the hybrid switch constructed by parallel IGCT and IGBT offers improved techno-economic performance as switching device in MMCs, by the decoupling of current conduction and switching between these two devices with respect to their strengths. Thereby, the electrical stress is redistributed between IGCT and IGBT, and the disparity between the static and dynamic breakdown voltages of IGCT can be effectively leveraged. Accordingly, an asymmetric voltage configuration approach for IGCT and IGBT is developed, and the voltage-related safe operation area of IGCT is expanded. The mechanism is validated by theoretical analysis and experimental measurement. As a consequence, the 4.5 kV IGCT achieves the voltage utilization ratio of 125%, and the power transmission performance of MMC can be significantly increased, suggesting a promising pathway for next-generation high-voltage power electronics.

item.title: Capacitor-Transformer: Electric Field Coupled 1-kV-to-400-V Conversion for Data Center Power Supplies
item.description: This letter proposes a capacitor transformer (CT), a nonmagnetic, electric-field-coupled converter building block that utilizes a stacked printed circuit board (PCB) structure to achieve high-density voltage conversion. By duality between electric and magnetic circuits, we derive a capacitive turns ratio model and integrate it into a resonant network to facilitate soft switching. A prototype utilizing a multilayer Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-PCB structure was constructed. It achieves a peak DC-DC efficiency of 97.3% and an outstanding volumetric power density of 26.4 kW/dm3, and a competitive weight power density of 15.38 kW/kg, delivering 9.6 kW with a 1000-V input and ∼400-V output at 1 MHz. These results demonstrate the system-level advantages of proposed CT, confirm it as a lightweight, core-less, and highly efficient alternative to magnetic transformers for next-generation data center power delivery.

item.title: Oscillation-Free Dv/dt Suppression for Medium Voltage VSCs With Series-Connected Modular Switches via Decoupled Linear Commutation
item.description: Voltage source converters (VSC) with series-connected devices offer a compact and low-cost alternative to modular multilevel converters for medium voltage (MV) applications but suffer from excessive dv/dt. While quasi-two-level (Q2L) operation mitigates dv/dt, it imposes complexity in both circuitry and control to prevent oscillatory overcurrent. This letter proposes an oscillation-free dv/dt suppression control for MV VSCs with series-connected modular switches. With a simple fixed-timing control scheme, output voltage stepping is decoupled from linear arm commutation, mitigating oscillations while preserving voltage balancing, modularity, and scalability. Experiments at 5 kV DC validate the targeted dv/dt suppression with minimized module capacitance among existing solutions.

item.title: A Capacitor-Assisted MPPT Buck-Boost Interface for Low-Internal-Resistance Energy Harvesting
item.description: While buck-boost energy harvesting interfaces effectively decouple source and load, their ability to match low-internal-resistance sources is inherently limited by the duty-cycle constraint, which imposes a lower bound on the achievable input impedance. To overcome this limitation, this letter proposes a six-switch dual-mode buck-boost interface featuring a capacitor-assisted maximum power point tracking (CA-MPPT) methodology. In the CA-MPPT mode, an auxiliary capacitor prebiases the inductor current prior to the source connection, thereby introducing an additional degree of freedom to extend the achievable input impedance matching range toward low-internal-resistance sources. Furthermore, a hybrid control algorithm is implemented to dynamically switch between conventional and CA-MPPT modes, minimizing auxiliary operation losses. Experimental results from a PCB prototype demonstrate a 30% reduction in normalized resistance mismatch, together with up to 7.4% higher input power and 13.5% higher output power.

item.title: AC Partial Power Processing for Bidirectional DC-DC Conversion With Wide Output Voltage Regulation
item.description: Partial power processing (PPP)–based DC-DC converters offer high efficiency and low device stress but typically require additional inverter and rectifier stages, leading to increased component count and degraded performance under wide voltage regulation. This letter first introduces the concept of AC partial power processing (AC-PPP), and based on this, proposes an AC-controlled bidirectional partial power converter (AC-BPC). By regulating the partial power ratio between the master and slave inverters through AC-side voltage control, the proposed converter achieves wide voltage gain range and load range. In addition, the AC-BPC eliminates the extra rectification stage, inherently provides galvanic isolation, and facilitates effective inrush current suppression, thereby improving power density and reliability. A 400 V CLLC prototype for on-board chargers validates the proposed architecture, delivering a 200–800 V output range and a peak efficiency of 97.98%.

item.title: A Novel High-Power, Wide-Range PT-Symmetric Wireless Power Transfer Systems With Reconfiguration-Free Arbitrary Current Ratio Adjustment
item.description: Parity-time (PT) symmetric mechanism has attracted extensive interest for their excellent positional robustness, but are strictly limited by the fixed current amplitude ratio determined by the inductance ratio, which greatly restricts power transfer performance. To overcome this issue, a novel PT-symmetric mechanism based on a specially designed S-0 topology is proposed. Theoretical derivations of the mechanism and parameter criteria are verified experimentally. The results show that: α is no longer limited by the inductance ratio L1/L2 and can be arbitrarily adjusted without hardware changes; and kc is independent of RL and positively correlated with α. Reducing α increases both output power and transfer distance. Moreover, kc can be used as a design parameter to determine α, ensuring high positional robustness under all conditions. When conventional PT-symmetric operation fails—large primary inductance and small secondary inductance—the proposed mechanism ensures stable operation while better balancing power, efficiency, and distance.

item.title: An Integrated Power Generation Center With an Internal Variable-Voltage DC Bus for More Electric Aircraft Application
item.description: This letter proposes an integrated power generation center and its associated control scheme for dual-spool turbofan-based more-electric aircraft. The architecture introduces an additional variable-voltage DC bus, which addresses two critical limitations: first, it removes the efficiency loss caused by field-weakening operation in the conventional high-pressure spool generator (HPG) at high speeds; second, it enables high-power resistive loads, such as electrical wing ice-protection systems (eWIPS), to be directly connected to a variable-voltage DC bus without an additional DC/DC converter. Besides these two contributions, the proposed control scheme also incorporates power control of the back-to-back (BTB) converter and reactive power compensation based on the instantaneous power theory. The letter illustrates this innovative architecture and presents a coordinated control strategy that simultaneously achieves field-weakening-free HPG operation, flexible power regulation for the eWIPS and stable BTB converter power control.

item.title: Simultaneous Wireless Power and Data Transfer System for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles Using a Three-Channel Magnetically Integrated Coupler
item.description: Both crosstalk between power and data channels and intensified common-mode (CM) noise in conductive seawater degrade the performance of simultaneous wireless power and data transfer (SWPDT) for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs). While existing solutions utilize discrete magnetic components, they often result in increased volume and mass. This letter proposes a three-channel magnetic integration method that achieves synergy through the co-design of the compensation topology and magnetic coupler. To mitigate interference, an XS–S compensation topology is employed to ensure load-independent constant-voltage output and robust CM noise suppression by balancing inverter-output port-to-ground parasitic capacitances. Furthermore, a three-channel integrated coupler featuring orthogonal flux paths is developed to ensure high magnetic decoupling. Experimental results validate the approach, demonstrating 1 kW power transfer at 92.7% efficiency, a 5.68 Mb/s full-duplex underwater data rate under 1 kW SWPDT, and experimentally verified CM-noise suppression compared with the baseline LCC–S topology.

item.title: Switching Loss Reduction Control for Interleaved Medium-Voltage AC/DC Converters
item.description: A key objective of the U.K.’s first medium-voltage DC project, Angle-DC, is to reduce network losses, with converter losses (around 10%) presenting significant improvement potential. To reduce the converter's switching losses, this article presents a power-routing based coordination control strategy for the interleaved input-series-output-parallel converter used in Angle-DC. The control method optimizes power redistribution across submodules (SMs), with high-power SMs operating at low switching frequencies to handle most power transmission, while a low-power auxiliary SM employs fast-response hysteresis control to mitigate the total current ripple, thus reducing losses without compromising output current performance. A hardware-in-the-loop test validates the proposed approach, showing a 38% reduction in switching losses compared to conventional power balancing methods for a full-rating system. The effectiveness of the proposed control is further partially validated using a laboratory-scale testbed.

item.title: A Novel Quasi-Constant Current Output LCCL Resonant Converter With Transformer Parasitic Capacitance Impact for HVCC
item.description: High-voltage capacitor chargers are critical components in pulsed power systems. To achieve constant current charge, the LCL-T resonant converter is proposed. Due to the non-negligible parasitic capacitance, the discontinuous secondary side currents cause output current attenuation as the output voltage increases, and the accuracy of the conventional fundamental harmonic approximation (FHA) model is affected. In this article, an FHA model based on continuous resonant currents is used to mitigate modeling errors caused by parasitic capacitance. Compared with the conventional FHA, the error is decreased from 12.7% to 3.9%. Meanwhile, this article proposed a novel LCCL resonant converter to compensate for the output current attenuation, achieving quasi-constant output current under fixed frequency while providing inherently short-circuit protection capability. Through the optimized design of capacitance ratio (γ = C1/C2), the LCCL converter significantly reduces current attenuation while maintaining zero-voltage switching (ZVS) across the entire operating range. Experimental validation on a prototype (Uo = 3 kV, Io = 0.4 A) demonstrates that the optimized design achieves an output current error of only 5.62% with full-range ZVS. Compared to the LCL-T topology, the LCCL converter reduces the maximum output current error from 17.9% to 5.62% while achieving maximum efficiency 95.8%.

item.title: A Family of Single-Stage Isolated Bridgeless Cuk PFC Rectifiers With Reduced Current Stress
item.description: Single-stage topologies are highly desirable in power factor correction (PFC) applications due to their compact structure and high efficiency. This article proposes a family of single-stage isolated bridgeless Cuk PFC rectifiers with reduced current stress. Based on the totem-pole bridgeless structure and the Cuk circuit, four modified switching cells (MSCs) are introduced, and a corresponding current decoupling pulse distribution strategy is designed for the MSCs. During the energy storage stage of inductors, this strategy can alter the freewheeling path of the output inductor, and decouple the common current path between the input and output inductors, which exists in the conventional Cuk circuit, thereby significantly reducing the current stress and conduction losses of the semiconductors. In addition, the presented circuits feature a simple structure and require a simple control scheme. The operational principle, design, and selection of key components, and the design of a dual closed-loop digital controller are presented under continuous conduction mode. Finally, simulations and two 250-W laboratory experimental prototypes are implemented to validate the feasibility and reduced current stress characteristics of the proposed rectifiers. Experimental results demonstrate that the prototype achieves a peak efficiency of 96.15%, a current THD of 3.35%, and a power factor of 0.997.

item.title: Power Synthesis Network for Megahertz Inverter System in Wide Load Operation Range
item.description: A novel power synthesis network and active modulation strategy for a megahertz inverter system in a wide load impedance range are proposed in this article. The wide load impedance range is converted into equivalent pure-resistive branch load impedances of two inverters by modulating the inverter input voltage ratio and output phase difference. In view of the wide pure-resistive load range, the load-independent resonant inverters are adopted for constant AC current source output and soft switching. Moreover, the constant AC output can be achieved by modulating the input voltages of two branches. To validate the proposed method, an experimental prototype operating at 13.56 MHz with an AC current output of 2.0 A was constructed, achieving a peak efficiency of 89.35%.

item.title: A Trapezoidal-Current Pulsed Power Converter Based on Modified Pulse-Forming Lines in SHINE Injector
item.description: Pulsed power converters are widely used in accelerator kicker magnets; they have different designs with different kicker characteristics. This article presents a high-precision pulsed power converter for driving lumped-inductance kicker magnets in the Shanghai HIgh repetitioN rate x-ray free-electron laser and extreme light facility (SHINE). The key technical challenges include the generation of a 250 A trapezoidal pulse current with a <1 μs pulse width, a 50 Hz repetition rate, and a <200 ppm pulse-to-pulse repeatability under a 1.2 μH inductive kicker magnet load. To address these requirements, a novel dual-pulse-forming-line topology is proposed. The proposed configuration mainly features two technical innovations: doubling the output current by connecting the kicker magnet to the electrically short side of the PFLs compared to conventional impedance-matched configurations, and accelerating the pulse leading edge by 30% through the utilization of a critical damping capacitor that maintaining waveform flatness. The mathematical models of the pulse-forming lines and the periodic pulse current time-domain expression of the kicker magnet were systematically derived. Owing to this, the key parameters of the pulsed power converter was designed and the charging voltage was reduced by 50% (from 12.5 to 6.25 kV) through impedance matching optimization. Additionally, a custom hydrogen thyratron with a 100 kA/μs current slew rate ensures precise switching performance. The experimental results demonstrate a 251 A output peak current, a 0.6 μs pulsewidth, a 0.76 ns (RMS) leading-edge jitter, and a 60 ppm pulse-to-pulse repeatability, which exceed the design specifications. The system was successfully deployed in the SHINE injector, enabling stable beam distribution. This article provides a scalable framework for high-precision pulsed power systems in accelerator applications, with potential extensions to industrial, polarization switching and medical pulsed power technologies.

item.title: A SiC mosfet Power Module With Copackaged Active Clamping and Cycle-By-Cycle Energy Recovery for Series-Connected Two-Level Converters
item.description: Medium-voltage (MV) two-level converters using series-connected SiC mosfets must simultaneously address device voltage sharing and turn-off overvoltage. In practical MV applications, the series connection of over ten devices renders a $\mathrm{\mu }\mathrm{H}$-level DC commutation-loop inductance unavoidable, causing severe switching spikes driven by the high $di/dt$ of SiC mosfets. This article adopts an active-clamping-based voltage balancing scheme and shows that the same clamping branch can also suppress turn-offspikes; however, the suppression capability is fundamentally constrained by the parasitic inductance of the clamping loop. To overcome this limitation, the main and auxiliary switches are copackaged into a 1.2 kV / 425 A SiC power module. By optimizing the clamping loop with a compact internal layout and an embedded PCB with local ceramic decoupling, the loop inductance is reduced to the 10-nH level. A transient model is developed to quantify the spike-suppression mechanism, followed by details on the module design, fabrication, and electrical characterization. Furthermore, a cycle-by-cycle energy recovery strategy is proposed to recover the energy absorbed by the clamping capacitor back to the DC bus, preventing voltage accumulation. A back-to-back experimental platform verifies the solution: device voltage mismatch is maintained below 5%, turn-off spikes are reduced from 180 to 24 V, and the clamping-capacitor voltage accumulation is suppressed from 35 to 5 V. The developed module can be externally connected in series for MV applications. Tests on a six-module series string under a 3600 V DC bus further verified the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Enhancing Overcurrent Capability of Power Modules With a Flexible Thermal Buffer Combining Heat Pipes and Phase Change Materials
item.description: Grid converters frequently encounter overcurrent (OC) conditions, necessitating fault ride-through capability to ensure reliable power supply. Addressing this challenge cost-effectively requires the implementation of transient thermal management strategies. However, significant difficulties arise from extremely high overload heat flux, slow response time of conventional thermal buffering approaches, and limited heat transfer capabilities of traditional air-cooled heat sinks. To overcome these limitations, this article proposes a novel heat sink with flexible dual-function thermal buffer, in which heat pipe array and phase change materials (PCMs) are combined to optimize the thermal buffering capacity and reduce the response delay without altering the original module packages. The proposed thermal buffer heat sink was directly attached to the metal substrate of a practical 1200 V/75 A IGBT module, and a low-melting-point liquid metal PCM was then used to immerse the heat-side of heat pipes for improved thermal spreading. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed module reduces steady-state junction temperature by 23 °C compared to a conventional module, enabling minute-scale sustained overcurrent capability at 2.7 OC, comparable to traditional synchronous generators. At 2.9 OC, the overcurrent ride-through time is extended from 0.19 s to 11.5 s. Simulation was employed to further elucidate the absorption mechanisms of the overload heat flux within the various components of the proposed module. A further benchmark indicates that the combination of PCM and heat pipes contributes to improved overcurrent endurance duration up to five times. For various fault current scenarios, this article delineates the safe operating areas for different heat sink configurations, providing guidance for practical applications.

item.title: A Synthesizable Digital Low-Dropout Regulator With Cycle-Averaged Ripple Suppression and Enhanced Transient Response in 65-nm CMOS Technology
item.description: To overcome the inherent power–speed tradeoff in conventional time-driven (TD) digital low-dropout regulators (DLDOs), this work proposes a fully synthesizable digital controller that enhances transient response and suppresses steady-state ripple within a dual-loop architecture. In the fine loop, a steady-state recognizer monitors comparator activity over three ripple cycles, while a voltage-positioning algorithm determines the optimal average number of active power transistors using cycle-based extrema. This strategy minimizes ripple and eliminates limit-cycle oscillation without dead-zone control, sigma-delta modulation, or extra comparators. The coarse loop incorporates on-chip frequency multipliers to generate high-speed clocks without high-speed external sources, and an undershoot-correction module that rapidly injects additional current upon droop detection. These techniques enable stable regulation with only a 10-pF on-chip output capacitor and compact silicon footprint. Implemented in 65-nm CMOS, the regulator supports a 0.9–1.2 V input range with a minimum 70-mV dropout, delivers up to 650 mA, and achieves an 80-mV droop, 5.2-ns response time, and 60.4-ns settling time for a 20–650 mA load step with a 30-ns edge. Occupying 0.146 mm2, it reaches a record 4.45 A/mm2 current density among TD-LDOs, demonstrating an excellent balance of speed and efficiency.

item.title: Design of Dual-Mode Buck-Boost Converter and LDO Integrated Output With Adaptive Seamless Mode Transition for AMOLED Displays
item.description: This article introduces an adaptive seamless mode transition technique between noninverting buck–boost converter and LDO regulator for modern active-matrix organic light emitting diode (AMOLED) display applications. Instead of pulse-frequency modulation (PFM) control, the system incorporates LDO operation under light-load conditions to overcome the inherent limitations of PFM control and maintains a clean output voltage, which directly affects display quality. By presetting an optimal control voltage and adaptively adjusting it through real-time feedback, the system ensures stable output and fast settling during mode transitions. To further enhance integration efficiency, the compensation capacitors and the error amplifier are shared across both operating modes, significantly reducing redundancy and minimizing silicon area overhead. Consequently, the proposed design achieves seamless transitions, improved light-load efficiency, and compact system integration. The chip was fabricated by 0.13-μm BCD process. The input voltage ranges from 2.4 to 4.9 V, while the output voltage is typically 2.8 V and programmable from 2.5 to 3.2 V. According to experimental results, the transition ripple remains around 5 mV or less with fast settling across the entire operating conditions, validating the effectiveness of the method.

item.title: Current Ripple Mitigation Strategy With Suppressed Neutral-Point Potential Fluctuation and Circulating Current for Parallel Dual Three-Level Inverters in Flywheel Motor Systems
item.description: To meet the stringent current quality demands of high-power flywheel energy storage systems, the parallel dual three-level inverter (PDTI) offers an ideal solution with enhanced output performance and power capacity. However, due to its inherent structure, the PDTI is inevitably affected by neutral-point potential fluctuations (NPPFs) and zero-sequence circulating currents (ZSCCs), which degrade the output current quality. To solve these issues, a carrier-based modulation strategy is proposed to simultaneously suppress current ripple, NPPF, and ZSCC in PDTI. This strategy begins by constructing a set of low-ripple and low-ZSCC candidate vector sequences (CVs) through an optimized vector selection and decomposition process, based on the developed principles of minimal switching difference, nearest adjacent/diagonal-angle synthesis, and centrosymmetric interleaved arrangement. Then, by dynamically allocating the CVs according to the corresponding neutral point current polarities, effective real-time NPPF reduction is achieved while preserving the advantages of low current ripple and ZSCC suppression. Furthermore, a direct carrier-based implementation is also proposed, employing modified reference signals and interleaved carrier patterns aligned with a novel sector division to substantially reduce the computational cost. Comparative experiments on a 500-kW PDTI-fed flywheel motor validate the proposed strategy, demonstrating its enhanced performance and practical suitability for high-power applications.

item.title: Identification and Localization for Inter-Turn Short Circuit Fault of PMSMs Using α-β Plane Voltage and Current in FOC
item.description: With the increasing power and torque density of the permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), the windings will experience higher electrical and thermal stresses, which may induce the interturn short-circuit fault (ISCF). This paper proposes a diagnosis method for the ISCF of the PMSM using α–β plane variables in field-oriented control. The mathematical model of the PMSM with ISCFs in the α–β plane is derived, and the usable fault features existing in the analytical expressions are analyzed. The second-order generalized integrator is utilized to construct the current residual that includes the short-circuit ratio and short-circuit current for identifying the ISCF. The polarity characteristic presents in the α–β plane voltage with different fault phases are explored. The fault factor for fault location is defined through the combination and logical operation of the characteristic variable. The processed fault factor is the DC quantity that is highly sensitive to the ISCF and is independent of motor parameters. The advantage of the proposed method is that the fault factor is derived from the analytical expression of the voltage and current inherently in the α–β plane, avoiding the iterative calculation, additional hardware, signal injection, and the establishment of specialized models for extracting fault characteristics. The experiment result demonstrates that the proposed method can identify and locate the ISCF, and has the robustness to sudden changes in operating conditions and parameter uncertainties.

item.title: Carrier-Frequency Square-Wave Voltage Injection Based Position Sensorless Control for CHB Converter Fed IPMSM Drives
item.description: In this article, the carrier-frequency square-wave voltage injection based position sensorless control is proposed for cascaded H-bridge (CHB) converter fed interior permanent-magnet synchronous motor drives. The carrier frequency of CHB topology is usually several hundred hertz to limit switching losses. It is challenging for implementing the high frequency (HF) signal injection for CHB converter using carrier-phase-shift pulsewidth modulation (CPS-PWM). For addressing that issue, the carrier-frequency injection methods, i.e., the preliminary injection and the advanced injection methods are proposed. In particular, the advanced injection based method employs a single H-bridge to generate the HF voltage injection signals separately, while other H-bridge modules modulate the controller voltage references with CPS-PWM modulation. Consequently, the modulation of HF voltages and controller references is decoupled, and the injection frequency can be increased up to the switching frequency (carrier frequency). In addition, a moving average filter with window length equaling the ratio of sampling frequency to injection frequency is proposed for position signal extraction, in such a way that the disturbances caused by harmonics in square-wave voltages can be eliminated, which fully utilizes the higher sampling frequency characteristic of CHB converter fed PMSM drives. Experiments are conducted to verify effectiveness of the proposed carrier-frequency injection based position sensorless control method.

item.title: Circuit and Operation of Multi-Pulse Auto-Transformer Rectifier Unit With Reduced Weight and Magnetics Requirement
item.description: A symmetric extended zig-zag 48-pulse rectifier unit ($\text{SEZ}^{2}$48PRU) is proposed for power quality improvement in aviation applications. The configuration upgrades a conventional 12-pulse zig-zag auto-transformer by integrating extended windings and employing a pulse-doubling circuit (PDC), thereby achieving a 48-pulse supply current. The resulting supply current exhibits a total harmonic distortion of less than 3%, ensuring compliance with DO-160G standards. The PDC diodes conduct a maximum of only 0.71% of the total load current, leading to significantly reduced conduction losses. A comprehensive design methodology for the auto-transformer is developed, including a step-by-step detailed description of its volume and weight computations. Theoretical analysis is supported by both simulation and experimental validation, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed configuration. Comparative evaluations demonstrate its advantages in terms of reduced %kVA rating, enhanced harmonic mitigation, and improved efficiency over existing state-of-the-art rectifier systems.

item.title: High-Order DC-link Ripple Characterization in Single Capacitor Multilevel Operation of Neutral-Point-Less (NPL) Inverters
item.description: Accurate evaluation of DC-link voltage and current ripple is critical for optimal component sizing to reduce volume and weight in high-power-density applications. Neutral-point-less (NPL) inverter topologies have emerged as attractive candidates for multilevel operation with a single DC-link capacitor, offering inherent neutral current elimination and ripple reduction potential. While previous studies validated ripple suppression in NPL inverters using low-order models and a single modulation scheme, comprehensive high-frequency ripple analysis across varying carrier-based strategies remains lacking. The absence of neutral-point voltage balancing in NPL inverters allows greater modulation flexibility, opening new possibilities for ripple reduction. However, this requires high-order modeling to accurately capture harmonic interactions at the switching frequency. This article presents a high-order analytical ripple model for the NPL H-type inverter, capturing switching-frequency harmonic behavior and enabling rigorous evaluation under phase opposition disposition (POD) and phase disposition (PD) PWM techniques. Results show that PD not only eliminates neutral current but also reduces DC-link capacitor rms current by up to 50% and input current ripple by 56%, primarily by doubling the ripple frequency relative to POD. These findings, supported by simulation and experimental validation, offer new insights into modulation-dependent ripple dynamics in single-capacitor NPL inverters.

item.title: Interleaved High Step-Up Isolated DC–DC Converter With Capacitor Voltage Balancing of the Dickson Voltage Multiplier for Micro Electric Propulsion
item.description: This article proposed a high step-up isolated DC–DC converter for micro electric propulsion application. The converter is based on the active clamp forward-flyback circuit and the Dickson voltage multiplier (VM) circuit. The VM capacitor voltage balancing and high step-up ratio are achieved through interleaved active clamp forward-flyback circuit, and the two transformers are primary-parallel secondary-series connected. The proposed converter structure also reduces voltage stress of power switches, and the volume of transformer. With the active clamp circuit, not only voltage spikes of leakage inductance are suppressed but also zero voltage switching (ZVS) for switches are obtained. Meanwhile, the switching losses can be reduced and efficiency of the converter is improved. In this article, the operation principle and steady-state analysis of the proposed converter are illustrated. A 100 W/25 kHz hardware prototype is demonstrated to convert input voltage 12–30 V to output voltage 2000–3500 V with peak efficiency 95.3%.

item.title: Dual-Mode Time-Sharing-Based Buck and Boost Inverter With Reactive Power Injection Capability
item.description: Among the numerous single-phase nonisolated single-stage buck and boost inverters (SS-BBIs), dual-mode time-sharing-based SS-BBIs offer significant advantages in terms of high efficiency, buck and boost operation, and low voltage stress. SS-BBIs with the neutral-point clamped structure can suppress leakage current. However, the main drawback of SS-BBIs combining dual-mode time-sharing technology and the neutral-point clamped structure is the inability to inject reactive power and achieve bidirectional energy flow. This article provides a single-phase nonisolated SS-BBI with a multiplexed buck and boost inductor and six insulated gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs) to meet the aforementioned requirements. In this article, the proposed scheme is justified from the perspectives of operating principles, mathematical modeling, design guidelines, control strategy, experimental verification, and comparison analysis. Finally, an experimental prototype with 500 W nominal power is employed in the experimental section to verify performance under the 110 V and 50 Hz AC grid.

item.title: A Compact IPT-Based Voltage Equalizer With a Symmetrical Interleaved Coil for Robust Drone Battery Balancing Under Misalignment
item.description: Inductive power transfer (IPT) multireceiver equalizers are employed to charge and balance drone batteries, but their effectiveness is often diminished by coil misalignment. This displacement leads to variations in mutual inductance between multiple receiving coils and degrades voltage consistency across the battery cells. To address this challenge, this article proposes an IPT-based voltage equalizer designed to be tolerant of misalignment by utilizing a multiple-winding receiving coil. The core of the proposed design is a receiving coil featuring a symmetrical and tightly-coupled staggered winding pattern. This structure ensures that mutual inductance among the coils remains consistent despite lateral displacement, preserving stable voltage balancing performance when the coils are offset. Furthermore, the integration of these multiple coils onto a single magnetic core significantly reduces the surface area, size and weight of the transmitter and receiver, a crucial advantage for drone applications. The proposed design was experimentally validated on a prototype for six series-connected ultracapacitors and batteries. The results demonstrated a balancing efficiency of 83.03% under perfect alignment. Importantly, the system maintained balancing functionality with an efficiency of 74.36% even when subjected to a 3 cm offset in the X-Y plane.

item.title: A Multifrequency Harmonic Suppression Approach for Eliminating Radiated Emissions in LCC-LCC WPT System for Electric Vehicles
item.description: This article proposes a generalized filter topology to fundamentally suppress radiated electromagnetic interference (EMI) in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems for electric vehicles (EVs). The proposed method is theoretically capable of eliminating harmonic currents by reshaping the impedance profile of the compensation network to block harmonic current flow at specific frequencies. A frequency-selective filter structure is introduced to target designated harmonic components, and this concept is extended for the generalized suppression of multiple harmonics, such as the 3rd, 5th, and 7th. Both simulation and experimental evaluations validated the effectiveness of the proposed approach under practical operating conditions. The results demonstrate that the proposed filter achieves significant EMI reduction, ranging from 18.2 to 24.3 dB, while maintaining acceptable system performance. The power transfer efficiency shows only a slight decrease, with a maximum drop of 1.6%, even when additional filter branches are inserted. Notably, the proposed method does not alter the system’s functional characteristics, such as input voltage or output regulation, which ensures seamless integration into existing systems. Due to its modular structure and design generality, the proposed topology can be applied to a wide range of WPT configurations, making it a promising solution for next-generation EV charging systems requiring strict EMI compliance.

item.title: Novel PCB-Embedded Inductor Structure for Resonant Switched-Capacitor Converters
item.description: Resonant switched-capacitor converters are very attractive for high efficiency and power density. However, the discrete resonant inductors limit power density of resonant switched-capacitor converters, which is one of major challenges. This article presents a novel coupled inductor structure based on an embedded magnetic core. The proposed coupled inductor structure features direct coupling technique, lateral flux and magnetic material with high saturation magnetic density, achieving lower winding resistance and higher inductance density. More importantly, the proposed PCB-embedded inductor can achieve 3-D-integrated switched-capacitor converters. The proposed PCB-embedded inductor is designed and tested in detail. The experimental results show that the 3-D-integrated switched-capacitor converters based on PCB-embedded inductor structure can achieve higher power density and efficiency compared with conventional counterpart.

item.title: Compact and Cost-Efficient Dual-Buck NPC Inverter with Dead-Time Elimination and Shoot-Through Suppression
item.description: Dead-time insertion is the conventional method for preventing shoot-through faults in neutral-point-clamped inverters (NPCIs), but it inevitably degrades output-voltage quality, lowers efficiency, and increases control complexity due to dead-time compensation. This article presents a novel transformer-based dual-buck NPCI (T-DB-NPCI) cell that inherently solves this problem at the topology level. The proposed structure not only eliminates the need for dead-time during normal switching transitions but also provides robust hardware suppression of destructive, long-duration shoot-through faults. The proposed cell is designed to safely withstand forced shoot-through faults of up to 2 μs, ensuring high reliability without requiring complex detection algorithms. The topology features a remarkably compact structure comprising only a single high-frequency transformer and one auxiliary diode, enabling all semiconductor devices to operate at only half the DC-link voltage. Furthermore, the streamlined structure allows for the use of standard sinusoidal pulse width modulation (SPWM), significantly reducing control complexity. Comprehensive results from a 425-W prototype achieving 97.4% peak experimental efficiency confirm excellent performance. A comparative analysis reveals an 8% improvement in power density (683 W/kg) and a 6% cost advantage over its closest counterpart. The T-DB-NPCI topology emerges as a highly promising solution where reliability and simplicity are critical.

item.title: A Novel Hybrid Capacity Regulating Transformer and Its Dual-Mode Flexible Switching Strategy
item.description: Distribution systems with high penetration of distributed renewable energy sources (DRES) encounter challenges such as inefficient operation under light-load conditions, and power quality disturbances. To address these issues, this article proposes a novel hybrid capacity regulating transformer (HCRT) integrated with an auxiliary multifunctional converter (MFC) and a dual-mode flexible switching strategy. An equivalent circuit model of the HCRT is developed to derive the arc-free tap-changing mechanism for both large- and small-capacity operations. Optimal energy-saving operating is achieved through coordinated control of the auxiliary MFC. During mode transitions, the auxiliary MFC fully supports the load and decouples tap-changing operations on both windings via solid-state switches, enabling zero-current, arc-free switching and simplifying the on-load tap changers design. A four-stage capacity regulation (FSCR) process is introduced to reduce control complexity. Furthermore, a dual-mode flexible switching strategy is proposed for FSCR, employing a weighting coefficient to coordinate grid-following and grid-forming control, enabling smooth mode transitions without inducing load voltage disturbances. Simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed topology and control scheme.

item.title: Modified Capacitor Voltage Balance Method for Novel Four-Level Nested Neutral Point Clamped Dual-Active-Bridge Converter
item.description: Due to the performance and cost limitations of commercial power devices, the conventional two-level and three-level dual-active-bridges (DAB), which are subjected to higher device voltage stress, face the challenges of a significant increase in cost and power loss under medium-voltage DC (MVDC) applications. Thus, a four-level nested neutral point-clamped (4L-NNPC) DAB is proposed in this article, where the voltage stress on each power device is reduced to 1/3 DC-link voltage. Compared with the existing three-level and four-level DAB, the proposed converter exhibits a cost and efficiency advantage in MVDC owing to its uniform voltage stress and reduced number of power devices. Subsequently, a capacitor voltage balancing (CVB) method based on modified redundant switching states (MRSS) is designed for the 4L-NNPC-DAB. Due to the independence of the MRSS in each leg, the complexity of the proposed CVB method is reduced compared with the other four-level DAB. Finally, the simulation and experimental results verified that the 4L-NNPC-DAB operates stably and controllably under the proposed modulation. In addition, the effectiveness of the proposed CVB method is verified in steady and dynamic output cases, disturbances of nonideal factors, and reverse operation.

item.title: Dual-Stage Coordinated Landing Control for Cross-Domain UAV-USV Fleets
item.description: This article addresses the challenging issue of coordinating multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to land on appropriate unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) in a distributed cross-domain setting. Although substantial progresses have been made for single UAV-single USV coordination, achieving seamless experimental cooperative landing of multiple UAVs on multiple USVs remains a dilemma. To this end, we establish a dual-stage coordinated landing control framework. In the first stage, a consensus-based auction algorithm (CBAA) is proposed together with a constrained minimum-energy task-execution strategy to enable pairwise UAV-USV matching and tracking. In the second stage, a visual navigation-based landing controller ensures smooth UAV landings onto designated USVs. Afterwards, sufficient conditions are derived to guarantee the finite-time stability of the associated closed-loop systems. Finally, both the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed scheme are validated through real lake-based experiments conducted on a self-established platform consisting of three DJI M100 UAVs and three HUSTER-37 catamaran USVs, along with simulations demonstrating its robustness to increased fleet sizes.

item.title: Variable-Gain Subdivision Pressure Control Algorithm for Digital Valve Braking Systems via Partially Open Mode
item.description: High-performance braking systems are critical for the safety and efficiency of the more-electric aircraft. Digital valve braking systems, employing highly reliable and robust on/off valves, offer a promising alternative to conventional electro-hydraulic servo valves, which are prone to contamination. However, the discrete switching characteristics of on/off valves often cause undesirable pressure overshoot, fluctuations, and discontinuities. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes a novel variable-gain subdivision pressure control algorithm (VSPCA), which leverages the partially open mode of on/off valves to achieve near-continuous flow regulation. The VSPCA comprises three key components. The sliding window-based state identification module accurately identifies the braking stages to enable stage-adaptive control. The variable gain regulation module exploits the partially open mode of on/off valve to improve regulation precision. Furthermore, a low energy consumption driving strategy is combined with a subdivided execution period to enhance excitation time resolution and reduce energy consumption. Extensive experiments conducted on a digital brake valve performance test bench and a ground inertial test bench demonstrate the superior performance of the VSPCA. Compared with the conventional PNM+PWM strategy, the proposed VSPCA achieves a reduction of over 50% in the average tracking error and other key performance indicators under triangular wave commands. It delivers high-efficiency braking under both full-load and half-load conditions.

item.title: Software Adjustable Mechatronic Inerter-Spring-Damper System
item.description: Mechanical elements such as dampers, springs, and inerters play a crucial role in various engineering applications, including vehicles, robotics, rehabilitation devices, vibration isolation systems, and structural designs such as seismic-resistant buildings and bridges. The ability to dynamically adjust damping, stiffness, and inertance in real time can greatly enhance the adaptability, stability, and overall performance of these systems. However, conventional methods for modifying these parameters often require physical alterations to material properties or geometric configurations, making them complex and impractical. This article introduces a software-adjustable mechatronic inerter-spring-damper system that leverages a permanent magnet synchronous machine, a two-level AC/DC converter, and control firmware to enable real-time modulation of mechanical behavior through electrical analogies. An experimental prototype is developed and validated, incorporating a sliding mode current controller to precisely regulate torque and achieve the desired dynamic response, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed approach.

item.title: A Novel Piezoelectric-Driven Small Bionic Underwater Robot
item.description: In deep-sea exploration, compact robotic systems encounter considerable difficulties in sustaining effective propulsion under tremendous hydrostatic pressure exceeding 100 MPa. Conventional electric drive systems suffer significant energy losses due to the requirement for large pressure-resistant enclosures, whereas current actuators utilizing functional materials frequently display constraints such as low thrust-to-weight ratios, elevated drive voltages, or insufficient pressure resistance. This work produced a unique seal-free small bionic underwater robot (SBUR) powered by piezoelectric actuation, inspired by anguilliform locomotion. A dual-SBUR system was employed to enhance the design of an underwater mobile platform (UMP), facilitating combined forward propulsion and steering capabilities. First, the structure and design of the SBUR and UMP are elucidated. Subsequently, modal features in air were examined by the transfer matrix method (TMM), while hydrodynamic responses in water were explored utilizing computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations. Performance evaluations conducted under standard pressure settings revealed a consistent SBUR forward speed of 152 mm/s. The UMP achieved a forward speed of 43.5 mm/s, generated a thrust of 20 mN, and performed rotations with a minimum radius of 130 mm. Experiments validated the practical feasibility of the SBUR at 110 MPa. The performance decline under elevated hydrostatic pressure did not surpass 3.3%, and it demonstrated negligible temperature sensitivity. Practical validation confirmed the SBUR’s ability to transport payloads of up to 10 kg, highlighting its suitability for deep-sea applications. The proposed robot offers advantages such as miniaturization, low operating voltage, and excellent adaptability to extreme hydrostatic pressure.

item.title: Position Prediction Control for Active Vibration Isolation System Based on LSTM Neural Network
item.description: This article proposes a position prediction control method based on long short-term memory (LSTM) networks for vibration isolation systems used in precision equipment. A dynamic model is established for active vibration isolation system, which is a six degree of freedom decoupled model as in practical applications. A position prediction control based on LSTM is designed. The design of position prediction control consists of two important parts, one is the network training process and the other is the control process. Unlike conventional LSTM neural network training, this work introduces robust quadratic regression (RQR) and Gaussian window techniques into the network. RQR is used to smooth the data trend. The introduction of Gaussian windows allows the network’s output to have a leading effect relative to the input and a slight filtering effect. The trained network is used in the control loop and combined with control gain to form position prediction control. Based on the dynamic model, simulation is designed, the proposed method is validated not only with proportional-integral-differential (PID) but also integrated with active disturbance rejection control (ADRC), demonstrating its compatibility and enhancement capability for feedback controllers. Finally, experiment is conducted using vibration isolation equipment to verify the effectiveness of the designed position prediction control. Simulation and experiment show that the noise is significantly reduced by the designed prediction control based on the comparison of power spectral density values. The control error is greatly reduced and the vibration transmissibility is effectively suppressed at key modes.

item.title: A Novel RUL Prediction Framework Considering the Long-Term Correlation of the Wiener Degradation Process
item.description: Remaining useful life (RUL) prediction is a core technology for equipment operation and health management, and the degradation process of most equipment generally has a long-term correlation. To address this issue, we propose a method to quantify the uncertainty of RUL prediction based on a temporal convolutional network (TCN)-transformer- Wiener Process (WP) degradation model. We give a new generalized RUL prediction framework that employs TCN-Transformer to fit device degradation trends, which can adaptively adjust the fitting of degradation trends. WP is introduced, and the concept of first hitting time (FHT) gives an approximate expression for the probability density function (PDF) of the remaining lifetime of the model, further quantifying the uncertainty of the RUL prediction results. Finally, verification and analysis on the bearing dataset and the tool holder power head degradation dataset, and by comparing with other existing popular methods demonstrated the accuracy and stability of the proposed RUL prediction method. This innovative method provides a robust solution for RUL prediction, offering significant contributions to improving maintenance strategies and operational efficiency.

item.title: A Bounded Nonlinear Controller for a Class of Step-Up DC/DC Converters: Application to Renewable Energy Sources
item.description: This article presents a novel time derivative-free bounded nonlinear controller (BNLC), including a refined integral action, for a class of boost DC/DC converters. The proposed strategy avoids the complexity of optimization-based methods through an explicit analytical form and mitigates potential noise issues associated with derivative-based controllers. The proposed controller guarantees boundedness via explicit duty cycle limits, with a complete closed-loop stability analysis provided. Validation on an experimental testbed compares the method against advanced proportional integral (PI) and sliding mode plus PI (SMPI) controllers. Results demonstrate superior and consistent transient response across the entire operational range, particularly in highly nonlinear low-current regions where it is 72% faster than robust benchmarks. In addition, it shows significantly higher noise immunity [12–20 dB higher signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)] and robustness under PV shading and large parametric variations.

item.title: Nondestructive Batch Testing of Mini-LEDs Using Flexible Electrode Arrays
item.description: Miniaturized light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are commonly used in displays and lighting for their high brightness, low power consumption and long lifespan. However, their small scale and high-density integration create challenges for efficient, nondestructive inspection, particularly for simultaneous electrical and optical characterization. This article presents a batch detection method utilizing a flexible electrode array that enables stable and simultaneous contact with multiple mini-LED chips. The flexible electrodes ensure reliable electrical connectivity and precise alignment with low contact resistance and nondestructive probing capabilities. Experimental result demonstrates turn-on voltage deviations within 44.83 mV, mean relative errors below 3.28%, peak and dominant wavelength shifts of less than 0.3%, color coordinate variations of approximately 2%, correlated color temperature fluctuations around 0.8%, as well as average radiant power and luminous flux deviations under 2.8%. The proposed method enables accurate, repeatable, and high-throughput characterization, providing a robust solution for the inspection of mini-LED arrays.

item.title: Primary-Side Control of Bipolar Field Excitation System for Pole-Changing Electrically Excited Synchronous Machines
item.description: This article presents a novel bipolar field excitation system for pole-changing electrically excited synchronous machines. Conventional wireless power transfer (WPT)-based systems realize bipolar field current through receiver-side active converters and wireless gate-signal transmission, which increases complexity and reduces reliability. The proposed approach employs a dual-input, dual-output WPT system with anti-parallel outputs and a self-driven MOSFET-diode configuration, enabling bipolar current flow without rotor-side active converters or control signals. This simplifies the system, reduces control complexity, and enhances reliability. A small-scale proof-of-concept prototype has been experimentally validated, demonstrating bipolar current reversal up to $\textbf{2.88}\,\text{A}$ ($\textbf{118}\,\text{W}$) with a self-driven rectifier efficiency of $\textbf{99\%}$ under an inductive load emulating the field windings.

item.title: Seamless Grid-Connected/Islanded Transition in Fully Grid-Forming PV Powered Microgrids
item.description: This paper investigates the full-state virtual oscillator control (fVOC) method to realize the flexible operation of 100% PV-powered microgrid. In the grid-connected mode, the enhanced DC voltage stability of fVOC enables the grid-forming PVs to provide the dynamic support while being compatible with the maximum power point tracking function. This dual capability is critical to replace the grid-following operation under the very high penetration of renewable sources. In the islanded situation, the reconnection of the microgrid to the bulk grid leads to large phase jumps, which can trigger the overcurrent saturation and induce the DC voltage instability in grid-forming PVs. To address this issue, a phase adaption current limiting method in fVOC is proposed. The full-order system modeling is performed to guide the parameter design of the proposed method. The Lyapunov function is derived to reveal the enhanced transient stability of the grid-forming PV system during the reconnection of the islanded microgrid. The superiority of the proposed method is verified in both simulations and field tests in a 100% PV powered microgrid. The mode transition between grid-connected and islanded is seamless without islanding detection and pre-synchronization.

item.title: Renewable Scenario Generation Using Quantum Generative Adversarial Networks
item.description: With the increasing penetration of renewable resources in modern power systems, scenario generation for renewable energy has emerged as a critical tool for system operation and planning. Among the widely studied methods, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are popular. Despite its model-free nature, it suffers from high computational demands due to its extensive trainable parameters and often struggles to capture intricate data patterns. To address these challenges, we employ quantum computing techniques that harness quantum entanglement principles. We propose, for the first time, a method of generating a quantum-enhanced renewable energy scenario based on a hybrid quantum GANs (QGANs) architecture. Specifically, given historical data of renewable resources, we design a quantum generator using parameterized quantum circuits. This quantum neural network achieves superior expressivity compared to its classical counterparts while requiring significantly fewer trainable parameters. Then, the resulting QGANs architecture combines this quantum generator with a classical discriminator for the effective generation of renewable energy scenarios. Quantum measurements also inherently introduce randomness into the generated renewable scenarios. The simulation results verify the rationale of the proposed method and demonstrate its effectiveness in capturing the spatio-temporal characteristics of renewable resources.

item.title: Central Controller for Photovoltaic Power Plants With Hybrid Energy Storage Systems Providing Automatic Frequency Restoration Reserve
item.description: Large-scale photovoltaic (PV) power plants have been increasingly used in ancillary service markets, providing services at significantly different time scales. These plants require additional flexibility to provide different services simultaneously and the solution is often sought in application of hybrid energy storage systems (HESSs). In HESSs, the power demand is shared between the storage units to maximise their performance and lifetime. However, this requires a detailed model of all storage elements that must be taken into account by the central controller of the PV plant. This aspect has not been yet studied in detail in the literature. In this paper, a central controller is proposed for a PV power plant with a HESS. This controller allows the PV plant to participate simultaneously in the day-ahead and the secondary reserve markets, providing both energy and power reserve services. First, the assets are scheduled in an optimal way, considering a horizon of 24 hours and taking into consideration weather forecasts and the expected balancing energy requests. Then, a short-term rolling-horizon optimisation with a time horizon of 30 minutes is used to consider the real operating conditions of the PV power plant and state of the storage units. The efficiency of each of the storage elements is modelled in detail for the short-term optimisation. The proposed controller is applied to a test case consisting of a 20 MW PV power plant with a HESS based on a lithium-ion battery and a redox-flow battery, each with 5 MW power and 10 MWh capacity. The main contributions are verified by using numerical simulations performed in MATLAB/Simulink for the electrical system and Gurobi for the optimisation procedures.

item.title: Cost Efficient Operation of a Hydrogen Enabled Zero-Carbon Multi-Energy System
item.description: Fully utilizing renewable energy with hydrogen storage technology provides a viable path to building a zero-carbon energy system. It is crucial to plan and synergize the operation of renewable, hydrogen, electrical, and thermal energy production, storage and utilization systems in order to minimize energy supply costs. This paper presents a coordinated long term (yearly operation scheduling) and short term (day-ahead and intra-day scheduling) optimization based operation approach for a hydrogen-enabled zero-carbon energy system (HZES) that incorporates renewable energy sources, multi-energy conversion devices, and long and short term energy storage. An integrated end-to-end prediction-optimization (IPO) method is developed to train the PV generation prediction model, which combines upstream renewable energy output predictions with downstream energy system operation optimization. This approach reduces operation decision gaps caused by renewable energy output prediction errors during training to enhance renewable energy utilization. The actual results are obtained from the HZES in production operation and the energy supply costs are compared with four other practical energy systems. It is shown that the HZES outperforms the current power grid and the city heating utility significantly in terms of the total annualized capital and operating cost by 37% to 49%. Furthermore, compared to the current rule-based operation process, the new optimization based method achieves more than 40% operational cost savings.

item.title: Degradation-Aware Real-Time Power Allocation Approach for Heterogeneous BESS
item.description: With growing hybrid configurations and phased deployments, rising heterogeneity in battery energy storage system (BESS) challenges the cost-minimization power allocation due to the difficulty in accurately quantifying real-time degradation cost. To address this, we propose a dual energy distribution model which stores the cycle information related to SoC trajectories within the energy distribution, tackling the problem to seek the full cycle for current action with past operation. A real-time degradation cost calculation formulation is presented to analytically express nonlinear degradation over a continuous action space, without requiring historical SoC backtracking or the rainflow simulation. A real-time power allocation algorithm is designed based on dual energy distribution ranking. Unlike traditional rainflow-based online methods that rely on estimating the marginal degradation cost of infinitesimal actions, our approach allocates power based on the degradation cost evaluated across the entire power range of each battery. Numerical tests and comparison are done for heterogeneous BESS response to frequency regulation service, the results show our method reduces daily degradation costs by 12% to 31% compared to conventional strategies, and with fast solving speed of each response within milliseconds.

item.title: Explanation-Enhanced Federated Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting
item.description: Accurate photovoltaic (PV) power forecasting is essential for the reliable operation of modern power systems. While federated learning (FL) preserves data privacy across geographically distributed PV stations, it suffers from performance degradation under non-independent and identically distributed data. Large language models (LLMs), known for their strong generalization and few-shot learning capabilities, offer a promising solution to this challenge. However, full-model fine-tuning of LLMs incurs considerable communication and computation costs in FL settings. To address this bottleneck, we propose a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework using low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules. While reducing overhead, conventional federated LoRA introduces aggregation noise due to the separate averaging of low-rank factors. To mitigate this, we introduce an explanation-enhanced unilateral LoRA paradigm that selectively aggregates generalizable modules while retaining task-specific ones locally. This design reduces noise, accelerates convergence, and improves interpretability. Extensive experiments on real-world PV datasets, using GPT-2 Small and GPT-2 XLarge, validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in improving prediction accuracy and training efficiency under heterogeneous data conditions.

item.title: Coordination of Wind Power and Energy Storage for Power Grid Frequency Support Considering Asset Degradation
item.description: Integrating energy storage systems (ESSs) into wind power plants (WPPs) can effectively improve WPPs’ dispatchability and frequency regulation support (FRS) capability. However, when supporting system operation, frequent discharging/charging ESSs and constantly changing the pitch angle of wind turbine generators (WTGs) would significantly affect the devices’ health. To find a trade-off between grid operation support and operation cost of WPPs, a hierarchical strategy is proposed. 1) At the plant level, a real-time coordination method is proposed to optimize quasi-steady-state (QSS) setpoints of WTGs and BESSs, considering devices’ degradation cost, nonlinear WPP FRS capability, and the ESS state of charge. 2) To mitigate WTGs’ dynamic fatigue load during the transition between QSS setpoints, at the device level, a model predictive control-based power control scheme is designed, which tracks real-time dispatch setpoints with reduced fatigue load. Simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Decentralized Coordination for Offshore Power Systems and UAV-Empowered Coastal Logistics Networks
item.description: The burgeoning electric-driven uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) technology is reshaping traditional coastal logistics dominated by cargo vessels (CVs). This revolution is catalyzing deep integration between coastal logistics networks (CLNs) and offshore power systems (OPSs) with rich marine renewable energy (MRE), ultimately evolving into sophisticated offshore power-logistics systems (OPLSs). Against such backdrop, this paper proposed a decentralized coordination framework for OPSs and UAV-empowered CLN to step towards a low-carbon and cost-saving OPLS. Firstly, a composite UAV-CV joint delivery model is developed to preciously capture energy- and logistics service-related characteristics of the CLN. Subsequently, by incorporating impacts of marine meteorological uncertainties (e.g., winds, waves and currents) on MRE generation and coastal logistics, a coordinative dispatching model for OPSs and the CLN is formulated to minimize total operational costs while complying with preset carbon emission caps. Finally, a decentralized solution procedure based on two-phase projection is designed for solving the coordination model to ensure decision independence and privacy security of OPSs and the CLN. Numerical studies confirm the effectiveness of proposed approach in MRE utilization, cost-efficiency, and decarbonization capabilities for the OPLS.

item.title: Reactive Power Coordination Control to Enhance Weak Grid Stability of Hybrid Power Plants
item.description: The rise of inverter-based resources (IBRs) and the retirement of synchronous generators weaken modern power systems by lowering the system strength. This leads to a lower short-circuit ratio ($SCR$) at the point of common coupling (PCC) of IBR facilities, resulting in weak grid conditions that hinder power transfer and threaten angle stability. To prevent angle instability, maintaining the PCC voltage at $1\,\text{pu}$ or higher is crucial, but injecting high active power into weak grids often increases the PCC voltage due to the high coupling impedance. Conventional voltage droop control ($QV-$droop) supplies reactive power by reducing the voltage at PCC, and compromises angle stability and contradicts the voltage requirement in weak grids. Hybrid power plants (HPPs), which combine grid-forming (GFM) IBRs (like energy storage) and grid-following (GFL) IBRs (like wind/solar), can enhance angle stability and ensure power transfer in weak grids. This study presents a dynamic reactive power sharing strategy at the plant level that uses GFM IBRs for PCC voltage control and GFL IBRs for reactive power support. The proposed algorithm was validated through EMT Simulations, demonstrating its efficacy under weak grid conditions.
